answers.answer: In The Great Gatsby, Jay Gatsby appears to be a rich and successful man but in reality he is a poor boy from North Dakota who made his money illegally. He throws big parties to impress Daisy but she doesn't love him back in the end. In Animal Farm, the pigs say that all animals are equal but they actually take over and become like the humans they replaced. Napoleon says he is working for the good of all the animals but really he is just trying to get power for himself. Both books show that things are not always what they seem and that people in power often lie.

Both Fitzgerald and Orwell explore the tension between appearance and reality as a central concern of their works. In The Great Gatsby, Gatsby's entire identity is a constructed appearance: his parties, his shirts, his title "old sport" are all designed to project wealth and sophistication in order to win Daisy. However, the reality — that he is James Gatz from North Dakota, enriched through crime — persists beneath the surface. Fitzgerald signals this tension through Nick's narration, which gradually strips away Gatsby's illusions.

In Animal Farm, the pigs construct an elaborate appearance of equality and shared governance, embodied in the Seven Commandments. The reality, however, is a return to hierarchy and oppression. Orwell makes the gap between appearance and reality most explicit when the commandments are rewritten at night, visible to the reader but not to the illiterate animals. Both texts suggest that appearance is actively maintained by those in power to serve their own interests, and that reality is only accessible to those with the means or position to perceive it.

Both Fitzgerald and Orwell construct their narratives around the systematic fabrication of appearance and the violence required to sustain it against the pressure of reality, though they locate this tension in different registers — the personal and the political respectively.

In The Great Gatsby, appearance is not merely social performance but an ontological project. Gatsby does not simply pretend to be wealthy; he attempts to remake himself entirely, erasing James Gatz in order to incarnate a fiction. Fitzgerald renders this through accumulation: the shirts, the parties, the invented Oxford past, the title "old sport" — each detail a brushstroke in a portrait designed for a single viewer. Yet the novel insists on the fragility of this construction. Tom's investigation into Gatsby's origins, Daisy's withdrawal after Myrtle's death, and finally Gatsby's solitary funeral expose the apparatus of illusion. Nick's closing meditation — "So we beat on, boats against the current" — suggests that the pursuit of appearance over reality is not merely Gatsby's pathology but the defining condition of the American Dream itself.

Orwell's allegory operates on a collective rather than individual scale, but the mechanism is identical. The pigs construct an appearance of revolutionary equality — "All animals are equal" — which functions not as a sincere political programme but as a tool of ideological control. The progressive revision of the commandments enacts in miniature the process by which totalitarian regimes rewrite history: the appearance of principle is maintained while its content is hollowed out. Squealer's role as propagandist — transforming defeat into victory, cowardice into strategy — demonstrates that the maintenance of appearance requires active, ongoing effort and the suppression of counter-evidence.

Both texts ultimately suggest that the tension between appearance and reality is not incidental but structural: appearance serves power, and reality threatens it. The tragedy in both cases is that those most damaged by the illusion — the working animals, Gatsby himself — are also those least able to penetrate it.

In The Great Gatsby, Gatsby hides his past as a bootlegger and pretends to be from old money to impress Daisy. He lies about going to Oxford and throws big parties to seem important. In Animal Farm, the pigs pretend to follow the Seven Commandments while secretly breaking them. Napoleon says he is working for all the animals but he is really just taking power for himself. Both books show characters who are not what they seem and who use lies to get what they want.

In The Great Gatsby, the green light at the end of Daisy's dock symbolizes Gatsby's illusion — his belief that he can recapture the past and win Daisy. However, the reality is that Daisy is shallow and ultimately chooses the security of her life with Tom over Gatsby's romantic vision. Fitzgerald shows that the appearance of the dream is more compelling than the reality of what it offers. In Animal Farm, Squealer's propaganda maintains the appearance of a fair and equal society while the reality is one of increasing tyranny. His manipulation of language — rewriting the commandments, reframing Napoleon's failures as successes — shows how appearance can be constructed through the control of information. Both texts suggest that appearance is not accidental but deliberately manufactured to serve the interests of those in power.

Both Fitzgerald and Orwell present the tension between appearance and reality as structurally central to their works, but they locate its operation in different registers — the romantic and the political — and assign it different moral weights.

In The Great Gatsby, appearance is a form of necessity rather than mere deception. Gatsby does not construct his persona cynically; he believes in it with a sincerity that Fitzgerald presents as both his greatness and his fatal flaw. The appearance of wealth, sophistication, and social belonging is, for Gatsby, the only available vehicle for a romantic ideal that cannot be expressed in any other way. Nick's famous observation that Gatsby's parties were attended by people who "conducted themselves according to the rules of behaviour associated with an amusement park" captures the fragility of this constructed world: it is real only insofar as everyone agrees to treat it as real. When Tom exposes the fabrication — "Mr Nobody from Nowhere" — the appearance collapses not because it was false but because the social contract sustaining it is withdrawn.

Orwell's treatment is colder and more systematic. Napoleon's appearance of benevolent leadership is not a romantic necessity but a calculated instrument of state control. The revision of the commandments, Squealer's statistical manipulations, the reconstruction of Snowball as permanent scapegoat — these are the technologies of a regime that understands appearance as the primary mechanism of power. Where Gatsby's illusion is ultimately self-directed, Napoleon's is directed outward at the population he controls.

The distinction is revealing: Fitzgerald suggests that the tension between appearance and reality is tragic, rooted in the human need to believe in something beyond the given; Orwell suggests it is criminal, rooted in the calculated exploitation of that same need.

In The Great Gatsby, Gatsby has a lot of memories of Daisy from when they were young and he wants to go back to that time. Nick tells him "you can't repeat the past" but Gatsby doesn't listen. This shows that memory makes him unable to move on. In Animal Farm, the animals try to remember the original commandments but they can't because Squealer keeps changing them. Old Major's dream is also a kind of memory of what life could be like. Both books show that memory is important but can also be a problem for the characters.

Memory functions differently in each text but in both cases it is shown to be unreliable and subject to manipulation. In The Great Gatsby, Gatsby's memory of Daisy is idealized to the point where the real Daisy cannot compete with his vision of her. His entire project — the mansion, the parties, the green light — is an attempt to recreate a past that may never have existed as he remembers it. Nick's warning that "you can't repeat the past" identifies the danger of living in memory rather than reality.

In Animal Farm, memory is a political tool. The animals' inability to remember the original commandments allows the pigs to revise them without resistance. Orwell suggests that collective memory is fragile and can be destroyed by those who control information. Benjamin the donkey is the only animal who seems to remember clearly, but his cynicism leads him to inaction. In both texts, the failure or distortion of memory has serious consequences — for Gatsby personally, and for the animals as a society.

Fitzgerald and Orwell both present memory as a site of distortion and control, but they approach it from opposite directions: Fitzgerald explores the tragedy of individual memory that refuses to release its subject, while Orwell examines the catastrophe of collective memory that is deliberately destroyed.

Gatsby's relationship with memory is the novel's central pathology. His love for Daisy is inseparable from his memory of her, and that memory has been so idealized over five years that the actual Daisy — compromised, conventional, finally complicit in Myrtle's death — cannot satisfy it. Fitzgerald externalizes this through the green light: a precise, observable fact that carries the weight of everything Gatsby projects onto the past. When Nick tells Gatsby "you can't repeat the past," Gatsby's incredulous response — "Can't repeat the past? Why of course you can!" — is not naivety but a statement of intent. He has organized his entire existence around the proposition that memory can be made real. The novel's tragedy is that it cannot, and that the attempt destroys him.

Orwell's concern is with memory as a collective and political phenomenon. The animals' inability to remember the original Seven Commandments is not a natural failure but an engineered one: Squealer systematically replaces historical fact with revised narrative, and the animals' lack of written records leaves them without the means to resist. The scene in which they stand before the barn wall, unable to recall what was written there, is one of the most chilling in twentieth-century political allegory. Benjamin's famous refusal to read aloud — "I could read it if I wanted to" — suggests that individual memory survives but is rendered impotent by passivity and cynicism.

Together, the texts suggest that memory is never neutral: it is either weaponized by power, as in Animal Farm, or weaponized against the self, as in Gatsby's case. In both, the failure to negotiate honestly with the past is fatal.

In The Great Gatsby, Gatsby wants to repeat the past with Daisy. He has been in love with her for five years and thinks he can get her back. Nick tells him you can't repeat the past but Gatsby doesn't agree. In Animal Farm, the animals forget the original rules of the rebellion because the pigs keep changing them. Old Major's dream is a memory of what life could be like but the animals can't hold onto it. Both books show characters who are affected by memory in different ways.

In The Great Gatsby, Gatsby's memory of Daisy has become so idealized over five years that it bears little relation to the real person. His "colossal vitality of illusion" — as Nick describes it — means he is not pursuing Daisy herself but a memory of what she represented. Fitzgerald presents this as both Gatsby's defining quality and the source of his destruction, since no real person can compete with a romanticized memory.

In Animal Farm, memory is not a personal burden but a political battlefield. The pigs exploit the animals' poor memories by rewriting the commandments on the barn wall and revising the history of the revolution. Squealer's revisionist accounts of Snowball's role in the Battle of the Cowshed demonstrate how those in power can reshape collective memory to serve their own interests. Where Gatsby is destroyed by clinging too tightly to memory, the animals are destroyed by being unable to hold onto it. Both texts present memory as unstable and consequential — its distortion, whether self-imposed or externally engineered, leads to tragedy.

Fitzgerald and Orwell both present memory as a force that shapes and distorts the present, but they approach it from opposite directions: Fitzgerald examines the tragedy of memory that cannot be released, while Orwell examines the catastrophe of memory that is systematically destroyed.

Gatsby's relationship with memory is the novel's central pathology, and Fitzgerald renders it with formal precision. Nick's retrospective narration — the novel is told from a point after the events have concluded — means that the reader experiences the story as memory from the outset, establishing a temporal instability that mirrors Gatsby's own condition. Gatsby does not merely remember Daisy; he has substituted memory for reality, constructing an entire existence — the mansion positioned to see her dock, the parties designed to draw her in — around the proposition that the past can be materially reconstructed. Nick's warning — "You can't repeat the past" — and Gatsby's incredulous response — "Can't repeat the past? Why of course you can!" — is the novel's pivot, the moment at which the reader understands that Gatsby's project is not merely ambitious but impossible by definition. Memory, for Gatsby, is not a record of what was but a blueprint for what must be.

Orwell's concern is with what happens when the mechanisms that preserve collective memory are destroyed. The animals cannot resist the pigs' revisionism because they have no written records, no independent means of verification, and no Benjamin willing to deploy his literacy in their defence. The barn wall — the physical site of collective memory — becomes the site of its systematic erasure. Each revision of the commandments does not merely change what the animals believe; it changes what they are able to believe, since the apparatus of belief has been compromised. Clover's moment of confused recognition — sensing that something is wrong but unable to articulate what — is Orwell's most precise image of what the destruction of collective memory produces: not ignorance but a kind of cognitive grief, the awareness of a loss that cannot be named.

Together, the texts construct memory as the ground on which both individual identity and political freedom depend, and its distortion — whether self-inflicted or externally imposed — as the precondition of both personal and collective tragedy.

In The Great Gatsby, the setting of West Egg and East Egg shows the difference between old and new money. Gatsby lives in West Egg because he is new money, while Daisy and Tom live in East Egg because they have old money. The Valley of Ashes is also an important setting because it shows the dark side of the American Dream. In Animal Farm, the farm is the main setting and it represents Russia during the revolution. The farmhouse represents the Kremlin and the animals represent different groups in society. Both authors use setting to show important ideas in their books.

Both Fitzgerald and Orwell use setting not merely as backdrop but as a means of encoding meaning and shaping the reader's interpretation of events. In The Great Gatsby, the geographical division between West Egg and East Egg reflects the social divisions of 1920s America. Gatsby's mansion in West Egg — ostentatious and newly built — sits across the water from Daisy's more established home in East Egg, and this physical distance mirrors the social gulf that Gatsby cannot ultimately cross. The Valley of Ashes, presided over by the eyes of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg, functions as a symbol of moral decay and the human cost of the pursuit of wealth.

In Animal Farm, the farm itself is a microcosm of a revolutionary state. As the pigs gradually occupy the farmhouse — the symbol of human power they had vowed to reject — the setting tracks the corruption of the revolution. Orwell uses the physical changes to the farm's organisation and the animals' living conditions to reflect political deterioration without stating it explicitly. In both texts, setting is dynamic rather than static: it changes in response to the moral and political conditions of the narrative, making it an active element of meaning-making.

Both Fitzgerald and Orwell deploy setting as a structuring principle of meaning, using geographical and spatial organisation to encode social, moral, and political commentary that operates independently of — and sometimes in ironic counterpoint to — the surface narrative.

Fitzgerald's tripartite geography — East Egg, West Egg, the Valley of Ashes — is a map of social stratification rendered in physical space. The bodies of water that separate these zones are not merely decorative: they mark the boundaries of a class system that Gatsby's wealth cannot dissolve. East Egg's "white palaces" and established aristocracy contrast with West Egg's "factual imitation" of grandeur, and the contrast exposes the novel's central argument that American meritocracy is a myth. The Valley of Ashes — "a fantastic farm where ashes grow like wheat" — is the novel's most concentrated symbolic location: the industrial waste product of the wealth celebrated in the novel's other settings, presided over by the faded advertisement of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg, a god whose vision has deteriorated to the point of uselessness. Nick's observation that the eyes watch over "a solemn dumping ground" identifies the Valley as the moral consequence of the parties in East Egg — the hidden cost of the dream.

Orwell's use of the farm as setting is allegorical in a more systematic sense: every spatial element maps onto a political referent. The farmhouse, initially forbidden to the animals as the symbol of human oppression, is gradually reoccupied by the pigs, and this physical transgression tracks the ideological betrayal of the revolution with a precision that explicit statement could not achieve. The barn, where the commandments are posted and revised, functions as the site of ideological production and manipulation. As the farm's infrastructure deteriorates under pig mismanagement while the farmhouse is furnished with luxury, the spatial disparity between the two becomes Orwell's most eloquent argument about the nature of the new regime.

In both texts, setting is not background but argument: the physical world is organised to make visible what the characters either cannot or will not articulate.

In The Great Gatsby, West Egg and East Egg are important settings because they show the difference between old and new money. Gatsby lives in West Egg because he is new money and Daisy lives in East Egg because she has old money. The Valley of Ashes is also an important setting because it is a dark and dirty place between the two Eggs. In Animal Farm, the farm and the farmhouse are important settings. The animals are not allowed in the farmhouse at first because it represents human power, but then the pigs move in. Both authors use settings to show who has power and who doesn't.

Both Fitzgerald and Orwell use setting to encode meaning that goes beyond mere backdrop. In The Great Gatsby, the Valley of Ashes — a grey industrial wasteland between West Egg and New York — functions as a moral counterpoint to the glamour of the parties. It represents the human cost of the wealth celebrated elsewhere in the novel, and the eyes of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg that watch over it suggest a faded moral authority presiding over a spiritually empty landscape. The geography of the novel maps social and moral hierarchies onto physical space.

In Animal Farm, the windmill is a setting that accumulates symbolic meaning as the novel progresses. Initially representing the animals' hope for a better future and Snowball's vision of technological progress, it is destroyed, rebuilt, and ultimately used for Napoleon's own profit rather than the animals' benefit. The transition from "Animal Farm" back to "Manor Farm" at the novel's close — the original name restored by the pigs — is Orwell's most economical use of setting as meaning: the circular return to the starting point encodes the novel's cynical argument about the inevitability of exploitation. Both authors use setting dynamically, allowing it to change in ways that track and comment on the moral trajectory of the narrative.

Both Fitzgerald and Orwell use setting not as a neutral container for action but as an active structural element that generates meaning through its organisation, its symbolic charge, and its capacity to change — or, crucially, to return to what it was.

Fitzgerald's geography is tripartite and hierarchical. East Egg, West Egg, and the Valley of Ashes constitute a moral as well as social map, and the novel's events are shaped by the rules of each zone. East Egg's "white palaces" house established wealth and the casual cruelty that comes with it; West Egg's "factual imitation" of grandeur houses aspiration and its inevitable frustration. The Valley of Ashes — "a fantastic farm where ashes grow like wheat" — is the industrial residue of the wealth celebrated in the other two zones, the place where Myrtle Wilson lives and dies. Fitzgerald's use of heat as a setting element in the Plaza Hotel scene — the stifling temperature that precedes and in some sense precipitates the confrontation between Tom and Gatsby — demonstrates how physical environment can be made to carry emotional and narrative pressure. Gatsby's pool, in which he waits for a call from Daisy that never comes and in which he is finally murdered, transforms a domestic amenity into a site of stagnation and death: the American Dream's final location.

Orwell's most devastating use of setting is structural rather than descriptive. The novel opens with the animals renaming "Manor Farm" as "Animal Farm" — a spatial and nominative act of revolution — and closes with the restoration of the original name by the pigs. This circular movement, in which the setting literally returns to what it was, is the novel's argument in its most compressed form: the revolution has not transformed the farm but merely changed who controls it. The farmhouse, initially forbidden as the symbol of human oppression, is gradually reoccupied by the pigs with each transgression normalised before the next is introduced. The barn wall, where the commandments are posted and progressively revised, becomes the site at which the relationship between language, power, and physical space is most nakedly exposed.

In both texts, setting is not what the story happens in but what the story is about.

Even though The Great Gatsby is set in America in the 1920s, its ideas are still relevant today. People still want to be rich and successful and sometimes they do bad things to get there. Gatsby's dream of a better life is something many people can relate to. Animal Farm is set on a farm in England but it is really about how revolutions can go wrong and how leaders can become corrupt. These ideas are universal because they happen in many countries and at many different times in history. Both books show that power corrupts and that people always want things they cannot have.

Although The Great Gatsby is rooted in a specific historical moment — the Jazz Age of 1920s America — its exploration of aspiration, class, and disillusionment speaks to universal human experiences. The desire to transcend one's origins and reinvent oneself is not limited to any particular culture or era, and Gatsby's tragedy resonates because it exposes the gap between the promise of social mobility and its reality. Similarly, Fitzgerald's treatment of the corrupting effect of wealth and the emptiness of a life defined by material success remains relevant across different historical contexts.

Animal Farm's universality lies in its analysis of the dynamics of political power. Although Orwell was responding to the specific circumstances of the Soviet Union, the pattern he identifies — revolutionary idealism corrupted by the self-interest of a new ruling class — has recurred throughout history. The mechanisms by which the pigs maintain power, including propaganda, the revision of history, and the exploitation of fear, are recognisable across many different political systems. Both texts achieve universality by grounding specific historical observations in patterns of human behaviour that transcend their original contexts.

The universality of both texts derives not from vagueness but from the opposite: their precise engagement with specific historical and social conditions generates insights that prove transferable across time and context, because the human and political dynamics they anatomise are structural rather than contingent.

Fitzgerald's novel is saturated with the specificity of its moment — the bootlegger economy, the Long Island social geography, the particular texture of 1920s excess — yet its central concerns transcend this particularity. The novel's argument about the American Dream — that it functions as a mechanism of desire that can never be satisfied because its fulfilment would require the abolition of the social hierarchy that makes it desirable — is applicable to any society organised around the ideology of meritocratic aspiration. Gatsby's tragedy is not that he failed to become a gentleman; it is that becoming a gentleman would not have been enough. This insight into the structural impossibility of certain forms of social desire is not historically bounded.

Orwell's allegorical method is explicitly designed for transferability. By abstracting the Russian Revolution into the grammar of a farmyard fable, Orwell produces a template for the analysis of revolutionary betrayal that has been applied to political movements on every continent. The text's universality is a deliberate formal achievement: the simplicity of the allegory — animals replace oppressive farmer, pigs become new oppressors — strips away the historically specific to expose the structurally inevitable. The commandment "All animals are equal but some animals are more equal than others" has entered the language as a formulation applicable to any system that maintains an appearance of equality while institutionalising hierarchy.

Both texts achieve universality through formal precision: they are so exactly true about their specific subjects that they become true about the general conditions those subjects exemplify.

Both The Great Gatsby and Animal Farm are about people wanting a better life. Gatsby wants to be with Daisy and to be accepted by the rich people in East Egg. The animals want to be free from Mr Jones and to have a better life on the farm. These are things that everyone wants today as well. The books also show that getting what you want doesn't always make you happy. Gatsby gets rich but he is still not happy, and the animals get rid of Mr Jones but they end up with Napoleon who is just as bad. These are universal ideas because they apply to everyone.

Although The Great Gatsby is set in 1920s America and Animal Farm was written as a response to the Russian Revolution, both texts engage with ideas that transcend their historical contexts. Fitzgerald's exploration of the corruption of idealism — the way the American Dream becomes a vehicle for self-destruction — speaks to any society organized around the promise of social mobility and material success. The gap between aspiration and reality that Gatsby embodies is not specific to Jazz Age America.

Orwell's analysis of revolutionary betrayal has proven similarly transferable. The pattern he identifies — in which a liberation movement is captured by a new elite that reproduces the oppression it replaced — has been observed in political contexts far removed from Soviet Russia. The mechanisms Orwell depicts, including propaganda, the revision of history, and the exploitation of loyalty, are recognizable across many different political systems and periods. Both authors achieve universality by grounding their specific historical observations in patterns of human behaviour — the corruption of idealism, the abuse of power — that recur across different times and contexts.

The universality of both texts is a formal as much as a thematic achievement: it derives not from vague humanist sentiment but from the precision with which each author identifies structural features of social and political life that prove invariant across different historical instantiations.

Fitzgerald's novel is so densely specific — the Long Island geography, the bootlegger economy, the Jazz Age social rituals — that its universality might seem paradoxical. Yet the novel's central argument is about the structure of aspiration in societies organised around the ideology of social mobility, and this structure is not historically bounded. The American Dream, as Fitzgerald presents it, is not a promise but a trap: the desire it generates cannot be satisfied because its satisfaction would require the abolition of the hierarchy that makes the object of desire valuable. Gatsby does not merely want Daisy; he wants what Daisy represents, and what she represents is inseparable from her unattainability. This analysis of the self-defeating logic of social aspiration is applicable to any stratified society that presents itself as open.

Orwell's universality is more explicitly designed. By translating the Russian Revolution into the grammar of a farmyard fable, he strips away the historically contingent to expose what he presents as structurally necessary: the tendency of revolutionary movements to reproduce the hierarchies they were formed to destroy. The simplicity of the allegory — which has been criticized as reductive — is precisely what makes it transferable. "All animals are equal but some animals are more equal than others" has entered the language as a formulation applicable to any system that maintains the appearance of equality while institutionalising privilege, because Orwell has identified the ideological mechanism rather than its particular historical manifestation.

Both texts achieve universality through the same method: they are so exactly true about their specific subjects that they become true about the general conditions those subjects exemplify. The particular is the vehicle for the structural, and it is the structural that endures.

In The Great Gatsby, Tom Buchanan thinks he is a good person and that Gatsby is bad because he is new money and got rich through crime. But Tom is also bad because he cheats on his wife and is racist. This shows that good and bad depend on who you are and what you think is important. In Animal Farm, Napoleon thinks everything he does is good for the farm but really he is just being selfish and cruel. The other animals think he is good at first but then some of them start to realise he is bad. Both books show that good and bad are not simple and that different characters see things differently.

Both texts present morality not as an absolute standard but as something constructed and perceived differently depending on social position and self-interest. In The Great Gatsby, Tom Buchanan positions himself as a defender of social order and tradition, condemning Gatsby's criminality while ignoring his own moral failures — his affair with Myrtle, his racism, and his casual cruelty. Fitzgerald invites the reader to question Tom's self-perception by showing us his behaviour through Nick's increasingly critical narration. Similarly, Gatsby is presented ambiguously: his criminal methods are clearly wrong, but his romantic idealism generates sympathy.

In Animal Farm, Napoleon presents every act of self-serving brutality as a sacrifice made for the good of the farm. The executions of the animals accused of conspiring with Snowball are justified as necessary for the community's protection, while Snowball himself is retrospectively recast from hero to traitor. Orwell shows how the concepts of good and bad are manipulated by those in power to serve their own interests. In both texts, the reader is positioned to see through these self-justifying constructions, but the characters within the texts largely cannot.

Both Fitzgerald and Orwell present moral categories as unstable constructions rather than fixed absolutes, exposing the degree to which judgments of good and bad are determined by social position, self-interest, and the power to control narrative.

Fitzgerald deconstructs moral certainty through the instability of his narrator. Nick Carraway arrives in New York presenting himself as a reliable moral arbiter — "one of the few honest people that I have ever known," he says of himself, with characteristic self-congratulation — yet his narration is shaped by affection for Gatsby and contempt for Tom that are not entirely rational. Tom's condemnation of Gatsby as a criminal is accurate but hypocritical; his own moral conduct — the affair with Myrtle, the careless disposal of her life, the racism dressed as civilizational concern — is equally corrupt. Fitzgerald's achievement is to construct a moral landscape in which no character occupies unambiguous ethical ground, forcing the reader to make judgments that the text itself refuses to make definitively. Jordan Baker's dishonesty, Daisy's complicity, Gatsby's criminality, and Nick's passive enabling are all present simultaneously, producing a moral atmosphere of pervasive compromise.

Orwell's allegory is more schematic but equally penetrating. Napoleon's genius — and the novel's most disturbing insight — is that he does not simply do bad things while claiming to be good; he reconstructs the very criteria by which good and bad are measured. When Boxer's maxim "Napoleon is always right" replaces independent moral judgment, the capacity for ethical perception is abolished rather than merely overridden. The animals cannot judge Napoleon as bad because they have surrendered the cognitive tools required for judgment. Snowball's transformation from hero to villain in the official narrative demonstrates that good and bad are not discovered but assigned, and that the power to assign them is the ultimate form of political control.

Both texts suggest that moral perception is never innocent: it is always shaped by position, interest, and the structures of power within which judgment takes place.

In The Great Gatsby, Gatsby does bad things like bootlegging but he does them for a good reason because he loves Daisy. Tom Buchanan thinks he is a good person but he cheats on his wife and is racist, so the reader thinks he is bad. In Animal Farm, Napoleon thinks everything he does is good for the farm but the reader knows he is really just selfish and cruel. The other animals think he is good at first because Squealer tells them he is. Both books show that good and bad are not simple and that different characters have different opinions about what is right and wrong.

Both texts present moral categories as unstable and dependent on perspective rather than objective standards. In The Great Gatsby, Nick Carraway functions as an unreliable moral arbiter. His famous judgment that Gatsby was "worth the whole damn bunch put together" is made despite his awareness of Gatsby's criminal activities, revealing that his moral assessment is shaped by personal affection rather than principle. Tom, by contrast, positions himself as a defender of social order while engaging in behaviour — his affair with Myrtle, his racism, his careless cruelty — that undermines any claim to moral authority.

In Animal Farm, Orwell shows how the definitions of good and bad are controlled by those in power. Snowball is transformed from a hero of the revolution into a traitor through Squealer's propaganda, demonstrating that moral categories can be assigned and reassigned by those with the means to control information. The sheep's bleating of "Four legs good, two legs better" at the novel's close encapsulates how perception of good and bad can be manufactured and imposed. Both texts suggest that in the absence of an independent moral standard, the distinction between good and bad becomes a tool of power.

Both Fitzgerald and Orwell present morality not as a set of objective principles but as a terrain contested by competing interests, in which the power to define good and bad is itself a form of domination.

Fitzgerald's deconstruction of moral certainty operates through the unreliability of his narrative perspective. Nick Carraway arrives presenting himself as a reliable moral observer — his self-description as "one of the few honest people" he has ever known is one of the novel's more ironic moments — yet his judgments are consistently shaped by emotional allegiance rather than principle. His rehabilitation of Gatsby — "worth the whole damn bunch put together" — is made in full knowledge of Gatsby's criminal activities and is best understood as a judgment about manner rather than morality: Gatsby's sincerity is preferred to Tom's cynicism, his romantic commitment to the Buchanans' careless self-interest. Fitzgerald suggests that in the world of the novel, "good" is whatever maintains one's social position and self-image: Tom's racism is presented as a defence of civilisation, Daisy's complicity in Myrtle's death is managed into irrelevance, and the Valley of Ashes — the moral consequence of the world the characters inhabit — is simply not looked at.

Orwell's analysis is more systematic and more chilling. Napoleon's achievement is not merely to do bad things while claiming to be good but to abolish the conceptual framework within which the distinction between good and bad can be made. When Boxer adopts "Napoleon is always right" as a personal maxim, he does not merely defer to Napoleon's judgment; he surrenders his own capacity for moral perception. Squealer's rewriting of history — the retrospective transformation of Snowball from hero to saboteur — demonstrates that "bad" is not a quality of actions but an assignment made by power, and that this assignment can be retrospectively applied to any action or person that threatens the regime's stability.

Both texts ultimately argue that objective morality is the first casualty of concentrated power: in Fitzgerald's world it dissolves into social performance, in Orwell's it is actively abolished.

Fitzgerald creates a convincing world in The Great Gatsby by describing the parties, the clothes, and the houses in a lot of detail. You can really imagine what it was like to live in America in the 1920s. He also uses real places like New York and Long Island which makes it feel more real. In Animal Farm, Orwell creates a convincing world by making the animals seem like real characters with different personalities. Boxer is strong and loyal, Squealer is clever and manipulative, and Napoleon is cruel and selfish. Even though it is about animals, it feels real because the characters behave like real people. Both authors are good at making their worlds feel believable.

Both Fitzgerald and Orwell create convincing fictional worlds through the consistency and internal logic of their settings, characters, and social systems. Fitzgerald grounds The Great Gatsby in the specific textures of 1920s America — the Long Island mansions, the New York speakeasies, the particular social rituals of the Jazz Age — and the novel's world feels convincing because it is rendered with sensory precision. Nick's narration gives the reader access to a coherent social geography in which the rules and hierarchies are clearly defined, even when they are being violated.

Orwell's achievement in Animal Farm is different: he creates a convincing world from the materials of allegory, which requires the reader to simultaneously accept the farm as a literal place and decode it as a political parable. The farm feels real because Orwell is careful about the internal consistency of his animal society: the pigs' greater intelligence, the horses' physical strength, the sheep's suggestibility are all maintained throughout and generate the plot's logic. The world is convincing not because it is realistic but because it is coherent on its own terms. Both authors demonstrate that a convincing fictional world is one in which the internal rules are established and maintained with sufficient consistency to generate belief.

The convincingness of a fictional world depends less on its resemblance to external reality than on its internal coherence — the degree to which its social, physical, and moral logics are established and sustained without contradiction. Both Fitzgerald and Orwell achieve this through different but equally systematic methods.

Fitzgerald builds his world through the accumulation of precise sensory and social detail that locates the reader within a specific historical moment with unusual vividness. The green light, the yellow cocktail music, the "enthusiastic meetings between women who never knew each other's names" at Gatsby's parties — these details function not merely as decoration but as the texture of a social world with its own codes and hierarchies. Crucially, Fitzgerald's world is convincing because it is internally stratified: the rules governing who belongs where, and what each location signifies, are rigorously maintained. The geography of East Egg, West Egg, and the Valley of Ashes is not arbitrary; it maps a social structure, and every event in the novel respects this mapping. Tom can never fully accept Gatsby not because Fitzgerald tells us this but because the world the novel constructs makes it impossible.

Orwell's method is antithetical in form but similar in principle. Animal Farm's world is deliberately stripped of naturalistic detail — we know almost nothing about the physical appearance of the farm beyond what is necessary for the allegory — yet it is entirely convincing because its internal logic is airtight. The hierarchy of species intelligence, the economics of food distribution, the mechanics of Squealer's propaganda, the psychology of Boxer's loyalty: each element generates the next with the inevitability of a theorem being proved. The world is convincing not despite its simplicity but because of it: Orwell has identified the essential dynamics of political betrayal and rendered them in a form that admits no irrelevant detail.

Both texts demonstrate that fictional worlds persuade not by resembling reality but by achieving a completeness and consistency that makes the reader feel the world could not have been otherwise organised.

Fitzgerald creates a convincing world in The Great Gatsby by describing the parties and the houses and the clothes in a lot of detail. You can really imagine what it was like to be at one of Gatsby's parties. He also uses real places like New York and Long Island. In Animal Farm, Orwell makes the animals seem like real characters with different personalities. Boxer is strong and loyal, Squealer is clever and talks a lot, and Napoleon is cruel. Even though it is a story about animals, it feels real because the characters behave like real people and have real problems like not having enough food and working too hard. Both authors are good at making their worlds feel believable.

Both Fitzgerald and Orwell create convincing fictional worlds through the internal consistency of their settings, characters, and social systems. Fitzgerald grounds The Great Gatsby in the specific textures of 1920s America — the Long Island mansions, the parties, the particular social codes of the Jazz Age — and renders them with sensory precision. Details like Gatsby's library, which contains real books with uncut pages, create a world in which even the props of illusion are fully realised. Nick's narration gives the reader access to a coherent social geography in which the hierarchies and rules are clearly established.

Orwell's achievement is different: he creates a convincing world from the materials of fable, which requires the reader to simultaneously accept the farm as a literal place and as an allegory. The world is convincing because Orwell maintains the internal logic of his animal society rigorously throughout — the pigs' intelligence, the horses' strength, the sheep's suggestibility are all consistent and generate the plot's development. The detached, objective narrative tone makes the absurd — talking pigs drafting commandments — feel logical and even inevitable. Both authors demonstrate that a convincing fictional world depends not on resemblance to reality but on the consistent application of its own internal rules.

The convincingness of a fictional world is a function not of its resemblance to external reality but of its internal coherence — the degree to which its social, physical, and moral logics are established, maintained, and allowed to generate consequences without authorial intervention. Both Fitzgerald and Orwell achieve this through the rigorous application of different but equally systematic methods.

Fitzgerald creates his world through the subjective, lyrical precision of Nick Carraway's narration. Nick's voice — sensory, retrospective, morally compromised — makes the world of the novel feel palpable because it is filtered through a consciousness that is itself vividly realised. The famous description of Daisy's voice as "full of money" is the novel's most concentrated example of this method: it is Nick's perception, offered as a quotation from Gatsby, that transforms a social observation into a metaphysical one. The world is convincing because Nick's consciousness is convincing, and the reader inhabits the world through that consciousness. The rules of the world — social, moral, geographical — are absolute for the characters within it, even when they are invisible to those characters. Gatsby cannot cross from West Egg to East Egg in any sense that matters not because Fitzgerald tells us this but because the world the novel constructs makes it impossible.

Orwell's method is antithetical in form but similar in principle. Where Fitzgerald's world is subjective and lyrical, Orwell's is objective and deadpan. The third-person narration of Animal Farm — detached, reportorial, free of editorial comment — makes the absurd feel inevitable by treating it with the same matter-of-fact tone as everything else. When the pigs begin walking on two legs, the narrative does not pause to register the absurdity; it continues as if this development were merely the next logical step in a process the reader has been watching unfold. This tonal consistency is the primary mechanism by which Orwell makes his world convincing: the rules of the allegory are so rigorously maintained that their implications feel inescapable.

Both texts demonstrate that fictional worlds persuade not by resembling reality but by achieving an internal completeness in which every element is consequential and the characters cannot escape the logic of the world they inhabit.
